speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel two with Alex Reifer. Reel two. OK. We were just talking in the break, and I was wondering if you had a radio in your house and-- or someplace where you could have heard things on the radio that you might not necessarily have heard. Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a radio, but we didn't work with the radio. We didn't tie up the radio at all. The only thing when we need to have some news, in the market, in [? central ?] market from the city, he was advertising, and he was telling over the microphone the situation, which it was in Warsaw, it was here, it was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean during the war or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before. During--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just asking you because I was real curious if there was-- if you attended movies, Yiddish movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw several movies. My parents didn't, because normally, very, very religious people don't go to movies. And I was with the maid several times to see Jewish movies. She didn't understand, but I-- I was. I saw-- I don't remember what kind of movies, but it was nice-- nice-- nice movies. Nice, Jewish movies made-- made in Poland. Poland has got 3 and 1/2 million Jews, and most of the Jews was religious, because the-- the atmosphere from the country in the-- in the bringing up the religion was religious people. You have got plenty of rabbis, big ones, Bobov rabbi, Belz rabbi, all kind of rabbis. Each congregation was for himself. It was-- it was a good life, a very good life. Simple and good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- again because you were religious, maybe you did not participate or go, but because there was so much Yiddish theater in-- in-- in Poland, Yiddish entertainers, comedians, singers, did they come to Oswiecim?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to Oswiecim, but whenever I've got time or I can get away from my house, I-- I-- I saw it. In this particular time, it cost 50 [? groszy-- ?] 50-- 50-- a zloty-- 50 [? groszy ?] is a-- is a half a zloty. Five zloty was a dollar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who some of those comics or singers might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Dzigan and Schumaker' was the two biggest comics not only in Poland, but in all over the world. 'Dzigan and Schumaker' was very famous, and I don't remember the rest of them. I-- I remember, but I couldn't remember now.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were growing up, do you remember encountering much anti-Semitism? What kind if you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland was very big anti-Semitism. I find in the school, in the [? Polish ?] school, in not only-- not only the school kids which I was in school with was anti-Semitism, but the teachers, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, they didn't respect you. I was in school-- I was in school a very tough guy. I was very strong, and-- and I was very intelligent. Not because I am, I want to tell you that, but I was. And the teachers didn't respect me. Only [? which ?] respect me when I complain one time when I was going to the director from the school, then he changed a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what sorts of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: They call you bad Jew, you know, different type of names. They call-- it was bit anti-Semitism in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- what kinds of-- how did you defend yourself against this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I must fight. When we was going out after the break, and they start fighting, and I must fight, and I always win.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was it difficult for you from such a religious family to fight? Did you feel any conflict?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was very strong, and I-- the friends of mine also was very strong. Can be also do a lot of wrestling when we was kid, 8, 9, 10 years old, you know? We was-- was doing different type of sports to protect ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you were the oldest brother, tell-- I'd love for you to tell me a little bit about your brothers. You haven't talked about them. If it's too difficult, I understand, but if you would like to, I think it would be nice for you to tell a little bit some-- a little bit about your relationship with your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: My second brother was a very intelligent person. I mean, he was a little bit older. He was not so religious as I was, and he was learning the-- the skill of diamond cutting and gold. And my third brother, Emanuel, was not only religious. He was fanatic. A lot of time, he didn't even talk to me because I'm not so religious as he is. This was my third brother. He-- he study-- he want to be a rabbi, but if he would be alive, he would be. And my fourth brother, [? Chaim, ?] was a young kid, you know, nice. And the little brother was always with me, I mean, whenever I go someplace, and that's all that it was. We was five children, and we was brought up very, very, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember, if anything, about the rise of fascism in-- in Germany? Did it-- did it even-- as Hitler came to power in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler came to power in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that? Did-- was it anything that your family even talked about, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I was-- it was on a Friday someplace, in-- in Oswiecim, I was in the center of the market. We're calling it a market, the center space. And I heard that Hitler [INAUDIBLE] that Hitler came to power in Germany, and this is the way it was. And we was talking about Hitler before that, too, you see, when he wrote Mein Kampf and all kind of stuff. He was that he hates Jews and all kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, we find out before that-- before that, and before 1933-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before 1933-- it was 1931 or something-- I-- I got-- my father's got a sister which lived in Berlin. Her husband was a lawyer, an attorney, and he came-- he-- he run away from-- from Germany, and he came to Oswiecim, because all family is in Oswiecim. And my father and my grandfather, he bring some money with him, but not too much, and he opened up a big store, a very big store, from-- from-- how do you call the peddle? Bicycle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bicycles, very big, and he was doing very good. And for this we know what happened. Friday night, we meet all of-- all the family met by my grandfather every Friday night. He was talking, talking, and he-- he told me that what happened in Germany, that Hitler came, and he took away a lot of stuff from him, and that's all. Sure, we know. We know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about since your family was, I would say, middle class, upper--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upper middle class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle class. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make a lot of tazedakah for people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do for the poor Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: She give a lot of money. My father give a lot of money. And my mother also wear a sheitel. You know what a sheitel means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: A sheitel is when-- when the-- when the woman gets married, they cut off her hair, and they put a false on top of it. It's calling a sheitel. She has got a dozen of them, different designs, styles. This is a way it was, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandfather was doing it. All the family was same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about on the holidays, or like on Pesach or on Shabbus, whatever? Did they make food for people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We give-- we've got in the city-- in the city we have got organization, a group, which my father was a part of it, and my grandfather, too, and my uncle when he came. And we put together a lot of money-- in this particular time, a lot of money-- and food, and we supplied the poor people with this food or with money. It was a lot of poor people in Oswiecim, too. A lot of them. And this what we did. Yeah. We help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you graduated from school, what happened next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is different what school. I graduate every couple years from a different school in the Jewish religion, which was-- I was-- in the Jewish religion, I was always on top of all the students which we learn. And I-- when I finish the school, I was 15 years old, the [? Polish ?] school. When I was before that, I always want to sell. I want to do something, and my father-- my father talked to a cousin of us, which had got one of the biggest haberdashery stores in the city, and he talked to him, and I was working part time. Also when I was even a little bit over 13 years old, I walked two, tree hours a day, and hour a day, you know, And this is the way it was. When I was already 15 years old, I-- my father talked to somebody to give me a job, and he find for me a job in a different city, Sosnoweic, Poland And the job was, because I am a religious person, done with-- with room and board. And the store, the name of the company, was Scoczilas, in Sosnoweic. Scoczilas. S-C-O-C-Z-I-L-A-S, Scoczilas. And he has got not the big store. He has got a medium store, or small or medium store, supplies for-- for manufacturers of suits and dresses, all supplies. I was working down there, and I don't need any money because I was going with him to the-- to the-- to the Shul all the time, and you know. Then all the money which I make, I sent it home. This is the way it was in our family, or in every family, I think, in Poland. Most religious family did that. And I build up the store, and I was going out to small manufacturers, medium manufacturers. I put in new customers. I was a kid, 15 years old. We were so busy that he must take a bigger store. And when he took a bigger store, I told him I would like to-- and I was already a buyer in this particular time, and I was sending to Bedzin. It's not far from Sosnowiec, this city, Bedzin, which we have big wholesalers from this particular part. And after that, he send me when I was already 16 years old, he send me two Lodz. Lodz is a-- is one of the largest cities in Poland. The first city, the biggest, is Warsaw, and the second city is Lodz. And Lodz is very, very big manufacturers. I was going down there to buy merchandise. Then I told my [INAUDIBLE], I told him we must hire somebody else, additional person, which he did. I got good paid, very good paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you about anti-Semitism in those years before-- right before the invasion of Poland. You were already young adult, nearing young adulthood. Do you remember-- can you describe for me any kinds of anti-Semitism you might have been experiencing at that point, if any?

speaker: interviewee
text: See-- Especially religious people was more tempt for-- for-- for this particular part. You have got people in Poland which didn't very pious. Only the religious people did. And the Poles was starting with this type of people which they are religious. They threw stones-- stones for the Jewish schools. They break windows in the Jewish schools. They break windows-- a lot of time, they broke windows in our house. This is anti-Semitism, and this particular anti-Semitism is-- is bring up by the parents from the children. The children didn't even-- the Polish children didn't even know what they're doing because the parents told them to do it. This is the way it was, a problem in Poland, a big problem. And Poland was 3 and 1/2 million Jews. It was a lot of Jews, and we got-- every city has got their own Jewish congregations, big congregations, you know. Like a mayor from the city, but he's not a mayor, but he is a mayor from the Jewish part, which the Jewish part help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Poland was invaded, where were you? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Poland was invaded, I was in Krynica, a city like Palm Springs here. I left Scoczilas, the business. It was too much for me. And I remember when I was young, my grandfather took me to-- I was seven, eight years old, nine years old. Every year they took me for vacation with him. He liked me very much. And-- and I remind myself this city, Krynica, is a beautiful city. It's warm always. Is nice. And I left to Krynica. When I came to Krynica, it was in 1938. And I look for a job, and I find a job in a hotel. The hotel-- I was-- they make me the manager from the hotel, and I was-- the hotel name was [NON-ENGLISH.] It's a nice, beautiful hotel, and I am-- with the hand I'm very good, you know, and I carve everything, and I make ice carvings for the parties every couple days. I make centerpieces. I don't know if you saw it, but from fruit I make. I make a lot of things. And I was liked in this particular job. They give me good service, good money, and that's all. And a couple days before 1939, we find out that the Germany will invade Poland, and I traveled to Oswiecim to see my family. And this is the way it started. When the Germans start attacking Poland, we left everybody, all the Jewish people, in Poland including us. We left everything. Only we take whatever can you take in your hands, and we left. And we left in a direction to Lwow. It's a little bit far, but this-- in this direction we was going, because in this direction, the Germans must come after us. This is-- Lwow was in the-- in the side of Russia, before Russia. This is the border from-- after Lwow is the border with the Russian government, the Russian state. And we left, and I was-- I was-- when I was home, I always take my grandfather with me because he didn't see, and he was going with me all the time. I was his eyes in time when I was in Oswiecim. And we was going. I couldn't go very fast, but we was going. And we came to a certain part after-- after, I think, a couple weeks, running every day. What we ate is I picked up from the fields, I picked up carrots, and I picked up potatoes, and we make some fire to-- on the outside. We was-- we was running, and this is the way we ate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't see bread a long time because we didn't pass any city. We only pass to the way to go straight how fast we can-- we can go. And we came to a certain place, and my grandfather told me that he couldn't run too fast, that I should go away alone. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because he said that the German, when they-- when they take people from 17, 18 years old, they kill them. That's what he said. And I don't want to go, but my grandfather said to go, I go. And I say goodbye to my family. I kiss everybody. And my grandfather put a hand on my head, and he [INAUDIBLE]. And I left. I didn't took with me nothing. I've got a little bit of money with me, zloties. I left it. I don't need nothing. And I left, and when I was running, and I run faster, I met a friend from our city. And his name-- I don't know he still lives. I hope he's still alive. Leon Klappholtz is his name, Leon Klappholtz. We calling him [? Lipe ?] Klappholtz. And-- and we got together, and we run together. And we run very, very, very fast. We was doing a lot of mileage daily, and we came to Lwow. We came to the city Lwow. When we came to the city Lwow, the Russian army was already there. A day before, they came in. They took-- the reason they took Lwow, because they don't want that Hitler should take it. Because they've got-- they've got [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a reunion [INAUDIBLE] I think before Germany attack Poland, that Russia will take Lwow in a certain part, and the Russian army came in and took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came-- when I came to Lwow, I didn't speak. I speak a little bit Russian, not too much, but you know. And I met friends of my father in Lwow, and he told me, Alex-- [? Issa, ?] what do want to do? He called me [? Issa ?] in this particular time. I said I don't know what I will do. I think I like to sell something. I said I don't have nothing. I don't have money. Money I will give you. How much you need? I said I don't know. What we can do, I say, we buy cigarettes from the Polish people, and I will sell to the Russian army soldiers. He said good.

speaker: interviewee
text: We make a price. I was a kid. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [DRINKS] And I was doing that, and I was working every day, running around between the Russian soldiers and selling cigarettes and taking ruble. And one time, we had that the Russian did [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] did register people to go to Russia. I couldn't stay in Lwow, nothing to do, and they give money. And I with my friends was going to register. And we register, we write up what we, you know, what we do. There is that, all kind of stuff, and they give us ruble to buy suits or something to give us. And they make a date. We didn't understand-- when we fill up the application, we didn't understand what we fill up. But this is no problem, and they-- they give us a date to be on the-- on the railroad station. And we came to this railroad station, and they put us in-- in-- in-- in big, big wagons. You know, the open wagons. Not open, but the cattle wagons. They put 40 people to a wagon. When you go in, it's a door. It's one side 20, and the other side 20. And-- and they put two floor-- like a two-- two floor. One floor this, up. [SIGHS] And they have got in this particular train, the whole train, was a lot of people. They've got a special kitchen. One-- one train was a kitchen, and one train was a medicine or medical. And that's all that it was. No bathrooms, nothing. No water, no bathroom, nothing. But they did have a day in the morning, they give us instead of 400 gram bread, which supposed to give us, they give us 200, because 200 you want to sell it to somebody else. And-- and they give us a soup, but the soup was water with something inside, and that's all that they give us. And they stopped twice a day to relieve ourselves. You relief yourself in the sides, whatever it is, and for eat, for food, this particular soup and bread. One time they give us 200 gram bread a day, that's all. And we came-- it took us a couple of weeks, we came to a place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a coal mine place. The name is Sorokino, was the name. Sorokino. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put us in a-- in a building before the [? shocks, ?] we calling it. And they put this in a room, and this room was seven beds. Was beds already down there, seven beds. Seven people was living in this particular-- each room. Was no running water. The bathrooms were outside. And they give us again money, and they took us to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means the grocery store, the big store. Now in Russia, it doesn't exist, if somebody wants to buy something in the store, he must be in the line a couple hours. It's not important what you want to buy, but because whenever you come, is a line. 50, 100, 200 people. Now with the privilege which we got because they bring us in here, that the-- the person which was going, it was a commissar. And he opened up, and he let us in before. This for the privilege that we can-- we don't need to wait in line. And we bought everything, and I tried already from this particular moment, I tried-- I bought different items which I don't need, and I sold it to these people who are staying in the line. [LAUGHS] All kind-- all kind tricks we did. We did-- And then they took us to visit the coal mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The visit, that means what kind of job we want to everybody can pick their own job. When I came to-- when I came down in the mine, I ask which pays the most money, and he said the most money pays [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means you couldn't go in like that. You must go in on the roller. You have a roller. You lay on the back, and you roll in, and then you chip the-- you put a mask. You have a mask, and then you chip to coal, and the coal was going out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this they pay the most money, and I was working down there. I made a lot of money. I made a lot of money, OK? The Russian ruble was a lot of money. And I was working down there many months, and my friend, [? Lipe ?] Klappholtz, also was there. When he was working, he don't want to go to this job because this is the toughest job what it is, and he was going to make streets in the coal mine. It's several hundred feet deep, you know? And make streets with dynamite and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- at one time, he was making a street not far from me, 20 feet or something. And I had something when I was knocking and I was moving out a little bit, I wanted to take a little bit more air, I had something-- something I had which is not normally when they blow up the street. And I roll out, and I see that the whole thing is-- you don't see any people. You only see stones. They covered. And I call a couple more people. They took off the stones, and my-- my friend was unconscious. He couldn't move. And he was got a-- got a broken leg, and they took him to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him to the hospital. I visit him every day, and-- and one time I came, I said to him, you know what? I leave. I wouldn't stay here. He said, [LAUGHS] where you going? I said I go to that city. The city is Voroshilovgrad. You must drive a whole night with the-- with the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, you could not move-- I don't know about now, but before without a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] mean a piece of paper who you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question because we're coming to the end of this reel. What was the name of this friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Klappholtz. [? Lipe ?] Klappholtz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I just wanted to clarify that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Lipe ?] Klappholtz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're at the end of this reel, so we're going to change the reel, and go to--
